{"id":"str-zwgc","title":"Test impact analysis: coverage-based selective test execution","description":"Implement test impact analysis (TIA) to accelerate local development by running only the tests affected by changed files. The system maintains a coverage map recording which tests touch which source files, then uses that map to select the minimal test set for a given set of changes.

## Design

**Coverage map** stored at `.shatter/test-markers/coverage-map.yaml`:
- Forward map: test → {file: blob_hash} (update direction)
- Reverse map: file → [tests] (query direction, derived from forward)
- Blob hashes via `git hash-object` for content-addressable comparison

**Query flow**: `git diff --name-only` → changed files → reverse lookup → union of affected tests → format runner-specific filter

**Update flow**: after test run with coverage instrumentation, parse coverage output → update forward map entries for tests that ran → rebuild reverse map. Atomic write via temp file + rename.

**Staleness tracking**: each file entry records the blob hash at recording time. Changed hash = stale = must re-run associated tests. Import-change detection: if a file's imports changed (not just function bodies), broadly invalidate its dependents.

**Cold start**: empty map treats all files as unmapped → falls back to crate/package-scoped testing. Map populates incrementally as tests run with coverage.

**Tiered test markers**: tree hash (from `git write-tree`) stored at `.shatter/test-markers/<tier>` after a tier's tests pass. Pre-commit hook compares current tree hash against the required tier's marker.

## Flags

- `shatter test` (or wrapper script) — run only affected tests based on coverage map
- `shatter test --all` — run all tests regardless, bypassing TIA selection (always available as escape hatch)
- `shatter test --record` — force coverage recording to refresh the map
- `shatter test --tier=<quick|standard|full|e2e>` — run the specified tier and write its marker on success

## Runner integration

- Rust: `cargo-nextest` with `-E 'test(=name)'` filter expressions (requires installing cargo-nextest). Falls back to `cargo test -- name1 name2` for module-level filtering.
- TypeScript: Vitest with explicit file paths or `--testNamePattern`
- Go: `go test ./package/ -run 'TestA|TestB'`

## Coverage collection

LLVM source-based coverage via `cargo-llvm-cov --json` for Rust (~10-20% overhead). Vitest `--coverage` for TS. `go test -coverprofile` for Go. For local dev loops where tests take 5-15s, always-on coverage is acceptable (~1-3s overhead). Alternatively, periodic recording with `--record` flag.

## Current test landscape (for reference)

- 1,487 Rust tests across ~60 modules + 3 integration test files
- 195 Go tests across protocol/instrument/generators packages
- 6 TS test files
- No cargo-nextest installed yet
- No cross-cutting test groups; filtering is by module/crate/package name
- Existing tiers: Quick (cargo test), Standard (+clippy), Full (+TS+Go), E2E (+e2e_concolic), Walkthrough (+demo script)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"33678+ketang@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-06T23:00:35Z","created_by":"Ketan Gangatirkar","updated_at":"2026-03-06T23:00:35Z"}

Mock return values are treated as explorable parameters with type-driven generation, mutation, crossover, coverage tracking, and behavior map recording. See docs/plans/ for full design.","notes":"Plan: docs/plans/external-dep-mocking.md","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"33678+ketang@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T23:26:30Z","created_by":"Ketan Gangatirkar","updated_at":"2026-03-07T23:26:30Z"}

{"id":"str-w0d.3","title":"Constraint complexity-aware solver timeout allocation","description":"The orchestrator uses a single solver_timeout_ms for all Z3 queries. Simple constraints (integer comparison) solve in microseconds; complex ones (string operations, nested quantifiers) may need the full timeout or be intractable. A uniform timeout either wastes time on doomed queries or starves solvable-but-slow ones.

## Approach

Classify constraints by estimated complexity before submitting to Z3:

- **Trivial** (integer comparison, boolean): 50ms timeout
- **Moderate** (string equality, arithmetic composition): 500ms timeout
- **Complex** (string contains/regex, nested boolean trees): full configured timeout
- **Intractable heuristic** (constraints involving opaque function calls, crypto): skip or minimal attempt

Learn from observed solve times: if constraints of a given shape consistently timeout, deprioritize similar future queries. A lightweight online classifier (rolling mean solve time per constraint category) is sufficient — no ML needed.

## Budget model

Total solver time per function is bounded. Instead of N queries × fixed_timeout, allocate a total solver budget (e.g., 10s) and let the scheduler distribute it across queries weighted by estimated tractability. When budget is exhausted, remaining unsolved constraints are marked as deferred for potential later attempts (e.g., by the hybrid fuzzer from str-e0uy).

## Why deferred

Requires instrumentation of Z3 solve times (not currently tracked) and a constraint classification taxonomy. The current uniform timeout works acceptably for the constraint complexity seen in practice. This becomes important when exploring codebases with mixed simple/complex constraint profiles at scale.","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"33678+ketang@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-07T23:44:39Z","created_by":"Ketan Gangatirkar","updated_at":"2026-03-07T23:44:39Z"}

{"id":"str-6aq.2","title":"Intra-layer function prioritization by expected yield","description":"Within a topological layer, the scan orchestrator assigns functions to workers in arbitrary order. When parallelism is limited (fewer workers than functions in a layer), high-yield functions should be explored first so that time-bounded or cancelled scans capture the most valuable results.

## Static signals (available before execution)

- **Branch count**: more branches = higher potential coverage contribution
- **Caller count** (from call graph): functions with more callers have higher blast radius — a bug in a widely-called utility matters more than in an isolated leaf
- **Parameter complexity**: struct/array/optional params suggest a richer input space worth exploring early
- **Dependency depth**: functions calling many other project functions are likely more complex

## Dynamic reordering (during execution)

If str-6aq.1 (dynamic budget reallocation) is implemented, per-function coverage velocity is already tracked. Use that signal to reorder the remaining work queue: promote functions that are finding new paths, demote functions that plateaued early.

## Composite score

Weighted combination: `score = w1 * branch_count + w2 * caller_count + w3 * param_complexity`. Weights are configurable; sensible defaults chosen empirically. No ML — a simple linear score is sufficient at this scale.

## Why deferred

The benefit only materializes when parallelism is limited relative to layer size and the scan has a tight time budget. In practice, most layers have few enough functions that all workers stay busy regardless of ordering. Becomes more relevant with farm mode (str-wiza) and total scan timeouts.

## Distinct from

- str-u7mr: prioritizes branch frontiers *within* a single function
- str-l22q: decides when to *stop* a function, not when to *start*
- Call graph topological order: determines which *layer*, not priority *within* a layer","status":"open","priority":4,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"33678+ketang@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-08T02:07:51Z","created_by":"Ketan Gangatirkar","updated_at":"2026-03-08T02:07:51Z"}

{"id":"str-18op","title":"Loop analysis: profile-guided prioritization","description":"Collect branch frequency data from the random exploration phase and bias concolic effort toward rare/uncovered branches.

Key components:
- BranchProfile struct: frequencies HashMap, rarity() method (1.0 for never-seen, 0.0 for always-seen)
- collect_branch_profile() in explorer.rs: count per-execution (branch_id, taken), divide by total
- Add rarity weight to FitnessWeights (0.15), redistribute existing weights
- rarity_boost on Frontier for priority adjustment (gated on profile presence)
- CLI wiring: profile available in scan mode (random→concolic), None in standalone concolic

Files: explorer.rs, orchestrator.rs, genetic_fitness.rs, frontier.rs, main.rs

Testing: unit tests for profile collection and rarity, proptest frequencies in [0,1], integration test showing profile-guided concolic finds rare branches faster.","notes":"See docs/plans/loop-analysis-compiler-techniques.md — Technique 4","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"33678+ketang@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-10T22:32:20Z","created_by":"Ketan Gangatirkar","updated_at":"2026-03-10T22:32:20Z"}

{"id":"str-7stb","title":"Input strategy decomposition and adaptive meta-strategy","description":"Replace the ad-hoc if-else input generation chain in explorer.rs and the separate worklist model in orchestrator.rs with a unified InputStrategy trait and adaptive meta-strategy. Each input source becomes a composable strategy struct with a next()/feedback() interface. A meta-strategy selects among strategies using outcome-based adaptive scoring with configurable cold-start protection, per-strategy floor allocation, and optional user-specified static weights. This unifies the two explorer paths under a single loop with different strategy sets.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"33678+ketang@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-04T21:03:16Z","created_by":"Ketan Gangatirkar","updated_at":"2026-03-04T21:03:16Z"}

{"id":"str-yvp4","title":"Add execution depth axis to genetic fitness function","description":"The genetic_fitness module scores executions on 4 axes (coverage, proximity, unknown_bonus, novelty) but none capture how deep into nested control flow an input penetrates. An input reaching branch depth 15 is strictly more valuable than one reaching depth 2 — it's closer to exploring the deepest, hardest-to-reach behaviors. Add a 5th 'depth' component: normalize branch_path.len() via asymptotic formula (len / (len + k)) and add it to FitnessWeights/FitnessBreakdown. This is a prerequisite for wiring fitness into the orchestrator.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"33678+ketang@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-05T02:45:57Z","created_by":"Ketan Gangatirkar","updated_at":"2026-03-05T02:45:57Z"}

{"id":"str-hoyl","title":"Wire genetic fitness scoring into orchestrator worklist ordering","description":"The orchestrator (orchestrator.rs) uses a BinaryHeap ordered solely by InputSource (UserProvided \u003e Z3Solved \u003e Fuzzed \u003e Seed). Two Z3Solved inputs are treated identically regardless of whether the parent execution reached depth 2 or depth 15. The genetic_fitness module exists with tested scoring logic but is never imported or called outside its own tests. Wire it in: when an execution produces new worklist entries (via Z3 solving or fuzzing), tag those entries with the parent execution's fitness score (including depth). Use fitness-informed ordering so the orchestrator spends its budget on inputs derived from deeper, higher-coverage executions rather than treating all same-source inputs equally.","notes":"Design note: orchestrator's covered_paths: HashSet\u003cu64\u003e (line 306) and FitnessContext.seen_paths: HashSet\u003cu64\u003e (genetic_fitness.rs:54) both track path hashes. When wiring fitness in, share state rather than duplicate. Also: WorklistEntry Ord impl (line 139-143) only compares source field — this is the specific code that needs to change to incorporate fitness.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"33678+ketang@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-03-05T02:46:03Z","created_by":"Ketan Gangatirkar","updated_at":"2026-03-05T03:14:34Z"}
